retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Conjunctivitis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal conjunctivitis is not an uncommon problem seen after delivery. When it occurs, the disorder is best managed by an interprofessional team that includes a neonatologist, infectious disease consult, ophthalmologist, pediatrician, pharmacist, and nurse practitioner. There are many causes of neonatal conjunctivitis. Ophthalmia neonatorum (neonatal conjunctivitis) presents during the first four weeks of life with eye discharge and hyperemia and is usually an acquired infection during delivery. If left untreated, it can lead to complications (ulceration and perforation of the cornea, blindness, chlamydia pneumonia). In severe cases, treatment should be initiated without waiting for culture results. Neonatal nurses will likely be the first to encounter this condition. They will also be responsible for monitoring once therapy has been initiated. Empirical treatment should be started soon after sending the culture and tapered once the final results are back. The outcomes for most neonates with conjunctivitis are good, particularly with interprofessional intervention and monitoring. [Level 5]. [14] [Level 5] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Conjunctivitis -- Etiology -- Neonatal Conjunctivitis
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal conjunctivitis, also known as ophthalmia neonatorum, is inflammation of the conjunctiva occurring within the first month of life and can be observed in up to 10% of neonatal patients. Conjunctivitis is potentially severe as this condition is transferred to the infant from the mother during delivery and differs from adult conjunctivitis because this includes ocular and systemic involvement. [14] The common organisms that cause neonatal conjunctivitis are Chlamydia trachomatis , Neisseria gonorrhoeae , herpes simplex virus 2 (HSV2), Staphylococcus sp , Haemophilus influenza , and other gram-negative organisms. Topical agents may also cause conjunctivitis, known as chemical conjunctivitis. The causative agent of infection can be determined based on symptoms. Staphylococcal infection can cause sticky eyes, while HSV2 infection may lead to watery discharge and vesicular lesions. Chlamydia can result in mucopurulent dismissal and pseudomembranes, while bacteria can cause purulent discharge. Finally, gonococcal infection may lead to hyperpurulent discharge and eyelid edema. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Conjunctivitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The age of the baby is an important clue towards the etiology of neonatal conjunctivitis; however, bacterial infections can occur anytime. The following is a summary of occurrences and causes. [6] [7] [8] [5] [8] First 24 hrs of life: Chemical causes like silver nitrate drops or from prophylactic medicines like erythromycin drops and gentamicin drops. 24 to 48 hrs of life: Bacterial causes are most likely (Neisseria gonorrhoeae is the most common cause, Staphylococcus aureus ). 5 to 14 days of life: Chlamydia trachomatis 6 to 14 days of life: Herpes keratoconjunctivitis 5 to 18 days: Pseudomonas aeruginosa

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Conjunctivitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of infectious neonatal conjunctivitis ranges from 1% to 2%.

retrieved_docs.title: Conjunctivitis (Nursing) -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial conjunctivitis is far more common in children than adults, and the pathogens responsible for bacterial conjunctivitis vary depending on the age group. Staphylococcal species, specifically Staphylococcal aureus, followed by Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenza, are the most common cause in adults, while in children, the disease is more often caused by H. influenza, S. pneumoniae, and Moraxella catarrhalis. [2] Other bacterial causes include Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis, and Corynebacterium diphtheria. N. gonorrhoeae is the most common cause of bacterial conjunctivitis in neonates. [1] Allergens, toxins, and local irritants are responsible for non-infectious conjunctivitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Conjunctivitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Erythromycin x 14 days or azithromycin 20 mg/kg/day x 3 days is the recommended treatment. However, the American Academy of Pediatrics still recommends erythromycin because other treatments are not well studied. A second course is usually required since 1 in 5 cases recur after antibiotic therapy. Pyloric stenosis has been seen in infants less than six weeks old treated with erythromycin. For infants born to mothers with chlamydia exposure, there is no prophylaxis recommended, but it is important to educate the family to monitor for infection, including pneumonia.

retrieved_docs.title: Chlamydia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Conjunctivitis: Direct inoculation with genital secretions infected with genital Chlamydia trachomatis serovars can lead to conjunctival infection. Symptoms typically include non-purulent conjunctivitis (erythematous injection of the surface epithelium). The conjunctiva may have a cobbled appearance. Conjunctivitis is the most common manifestation of chlamydial infection in newborns. Pneumonia: Infants born to mothers who have a cervical infection with Chlamydia trachomatis may develop pneumonia 5-30% of the time. [8] Recognition usually occurs between 4 to 12 weeks of age, though almost all infants have symptoms before eight weeks. Nasal congestion and cough are common, and some infants may have thick nasal secretions. [9] Infants usually have no fever or only a minimal fever, they may have tachypnea, and they may have a characteristic paroxysmal staccato cough. In preterm infants, apneic spells may be seen. Rales may be heard on auscultation of the lungs, but wheezing is uncommon.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Conjunctivitis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Laterality, unilateral conjunctivitis is most often seen with S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, Herpes simplex , and adenovirus. Bilateral conjunctivitis is seen with infection caused by N. gonorrhea or by use of ocular prophylaxis. Chlamydia usually develops in one eye but affects the other after 2 to 7 days. The physical exam should include evaluation for red reflex, ulcerations (corneal and conjunctival), and adenopathy. One should also rule out signs of respiratory and systemic infection. A history of sexually transmitted infection in the mother increases the risk of chlamydia and gonorrhea. Neonatal conjunctivitis is frequently diagnosed in neonates born to HIV-infected mothers. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Keratoconjunctivitis -- Etiology -- Infectious Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Common bacterial pathogens that give rise to acute bacterial keratoconjunctivitis include Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pneumoniae . More severe infections by Pseudomonas aeruginosa are prevalent in contact lens wearers and can rapidly lead to corneal perforation if untreated. Neonatal keratoconjunctivitis, often caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae or Chlamydia trachomatis , presents within the first few days of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Nongonococcal Urethritis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Children born to mothers with chlamydia urethritis can potentially develop complications including conjunctivitis, iritis, and pneumonia. Routine treatment of all newborns with antibiotic eye ointment has significantly decreased the incidence of these. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Conjunctivitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Chlamydial conjunctivitis: Recommended prophylaxis does not prevent chlamydial conjunctivitis, and topical treatment is ineffective and unnecessary but may be used as an adjunct to oral therapy. Topical therapy for chlamydial conjunctivitis is generally not effective and has a high failure rate because C. trachomatis is known to colonize the nasopharynx and can thus spread into the lungs if not adequately eradicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Conjunctivitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal eye discharge is usually due to congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction or either chemical or infectious conjunctivitis. Neonatal conjunctivitis, also called ophthalmia neonatorum, typically presents during the first four weeks of life. The infection is usually acquired during delivery and is the most common ocular disease in neonates. Typical symptoms are persistent tearing and a mucoid discharge in the inner corner of the eye. This activity examines the presentation, evaluation, and management of neonatal conjunctivitis and stresses the role of an interprofessional team approach to the care of affected neonates. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Conjunctivitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors include premature rupture of membranes, prolonged delivery, prematurity, poor prenatal care, maternal STI, mechanical ventilation, poor hygiene conditions, history of midwife assistance, and HIV-infected mothers.

retrieved_docs.title: Conjunctivitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial conjunctivitis is far more common in children than adults, and the pathogens responsible for bacterial conjunctivitis vary depending on the affected child’s age group. Staphylococcal species, specifically Staphylococcus aureus , followed by Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenza , are the most common bacterial causes in adults. [7] However, the disease is more often caused by Haemophilus influenza , Streptococcus pneumoniae , and Moraxella catarrhalis in children. [6] Other bacterial causes include Neisseria gonorrhoeae , Chlamydia trachomatis , and Corynebacterium diphtheria . Neisseria gonorrhoeae is the most common cause of bacterial conjunctivitis in neonates and sexually active adults. [8] Allergens, toxins, and local irritants are responsible for noninfectious conjunctivitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Ophthalmia Neonatorum -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Before 1880, ophthalmia neonatorum due to Neisseria gonorrhoeae was the most common cause of neonatal blindness. Early detection and timely treatment of infected mothers are crucial elements in preventing permanent ocular damage. If left untreated or partially treated, corneal ulceration, perforation, and blindness can occur. Approximately 10000 cases of blindness per year are secondary to ophthalmia neonatorum worldwide. Fortunately, in most cases, neonatal ophthalmia neonatorum caused by nongonococcal bacteria is a mild disease with a good prognosis. However, up to 50% of babies born to mothers with chlamydia infection may develop neonatal conjunctivitis, and among those, between 10% and 20% are at risk of developing pneumonia. Chemical conjunctivitis secondary to silver nitrate is self-limiting. [56] [51] [83]

retrieved_docs.title: Conjunctivitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of acute conjunctivitis are rare. However, patients who fail to show improvement within 5 to 7 days should have a referral to an ophthalmologist for further evaluation. Patients with herpes simplex conjunctivitis are at the highest risk of complications. Approximately 38.2% of patients with herpes simplex virus develop corneal complications, and 19.1% develop uveitis; these patients should always see an ophthalmologist for close re-evaluation. [47] Patients with Neisseria gonorrhoeae are also at high risk for corneal involvement and secondary corneal perforation and should be treated appropriately. Chlamydial conjunctivitis in the newborn can cause pneumonia and otitis media. [62]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza, referred to in Arabic as الالتهاب الرئوي, is a viral respiratory infection that can lead to or be associated with pneumonia. The broader term الالتهاب الرئوي also includes specific forms such as التهاب رئوي وليدي, which denotes neonatal pneumonia, a distinct subtype of pneumonia occurring in newborn infants. Understanding that influenza may precipitate pneumonia and that neonatal pneumonia is a particular manifestation of pneumonia aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, especially infants, and in applying appropriate preventive and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1625
retrieved_docs.content: التهاب رئوي وليدي (neonatal pneumonia) is identified as a child or specific subtype of الالتهاب رِئَوِيّ (pneumonia), indicating that neonatal pneumonia falls under the broader category of pneumonia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: ocular inflammation or infection, meningitis, and cases with a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية). Within disease hierarchies, inflammation or infection of the eye functions as a parent category that includes bacterial conjunctivitis (التهاب الملتحمة الجرثومي) and ocular tularaemia (التولاريمية); meningitis serves as the broader category under which bacterial meningitis (التهاب السحايا الجرثومي) is classified. These β‑lactam antibiotics therefore serve both preventive and therapeutic roles across a spectrum of bacterial disease presentations, from localized middle‑ear infections to serious systemic conditions such as bacterial meningitis and ocular infections. Their use requires careful drug selection, awareness of contraindications, and monitoring for adverse drug effects, particularly allergic reactions and other β‑lactam‑related toxicities, to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: is a respiratory viral infection that can predispose to secondary fungal infection, specifically coccidioidal pneumonia, and is linked to pulmonary disease. Positive tuberculin testing is associated with pulmonary infections such as pneumonia and bacterial pneumonia and is also connected to cranial measurement assessments in evaluating both respiratory and extrapulmonary disease. Pediatric pneumonia represents an age‑specific subtype of general pneumonia. Salmonellosis serves as a parent condition for infective arthritis, osteomyelitis and pneumonia, illustrating its potential for diverse infectious sequelae. Ocular inflammation/infection is the parent category for bacterial conjunctivitis and tularaemia, indicating a common inflammatory‑infectious pathway for these eye conditions. The Arabic term الالتهاب الرئوي الجرثومي corresponds to bacterial pneumonia, reinforcing the equivalence of English and Arabic nomenclature for these diseases. Together, the hierarchical relationships among

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1552
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a type of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder). The broader category اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة includes the specific conditions كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة. Virologie is an allowed qualifier for اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, for respiratory distress syndrome, and also for كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة, indicating that viral factors may be relevant in the assessment and management of these neonatal disorders. This hierarchical organization links respiratory distress syndrome to other neonatal conditions and emphasizes the potential role of virology across the spectrum of neonatal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 695
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified as a subtype of الْتِهابِ شعبي and is clinically associated with الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ (pulmonary inflammation). Influenza is regarded as a form of الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ, and it is also listed as a child of التهاب رئوي بالعقديات (coccal pneumonia), reflecting that viral influenza can predispose to or coexist with bacterial pulmonary infection. The pediatric condition التهاب رئوي وليدي (neonatal pulmonary inflammation) is a child of الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ, indicating it is a specific neonatal variant of pulmonary inflammation. Gemifloxacin may be used to treat التهاب رئوي بالعقديات, highlighting its role as an antibiotic effective against coccal pneumonia. Recognizing these hierarchical and clinical relationships helps clinicians understand how acute bronchitis, influenza, and various forms of pneumonia are interconnected and guides appropriate therapeutic decisions, such as employing gemifloxacin for bacterial lung infections while being aware of the potential for viral infections like influenza to complicate or trigger secondary bacterial pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.content: reinforce the connection between neonatal respiratory compromise, extreme prematurity, and perinatal infection. This integrated hierarchy supports comprehensive patient care by aligning gestational age, birth weight, fetal growth, and respiratory complications with their appropriate diagnostic categories across ICD‑10‑AM, WHO perinatal codes, and multilingual clinical terminologies.

retrieved_docs.content: رئوي وليدي) is also a child condition of the broader pulmonary inflammation category. Both general pulmonary inflammation and bacterial pneumonia are frequently associated with a positive tuberculin skin test (فحص التوبركولين إيجابي), a finding similarly observed in skull enlargement (حج القحف), suggesting overlapping immunologic or infectious markers. Bacterial pneumonia possesses a transmissible component (přenos), underscoring the need for infection‑control measures when managing affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1508
retrieved_docs.content: التهاب رئوي وليدي, a neonatal form of الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ (pneumonia), is considered a specific child subtype of pneumonia. In clinical management, sucralfate (sukralfát) may be used as a therapeutic option for الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ, indicating its potential role in treating this respiratory infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Ciprofloxacin serves an antibacterial therapeutic role and is commonly employed to treat bacterial conjunctivitis (التهاب الملتحمة الجرثومي).
